comments.author: @bewustsein
comments.text:
Danke für diese sehr Umfassende Doku über mein Land. Es ist doch immer interresant zu sehen, wie ander Länder über uns denken. Und ich bin erstaunt - sehr Positiv. Und ja, wir haben wirklich viel zu viele Regeln -  ich glaube, das liegt daran, das wir Deutsche alle kleine Hosenschisser sind und gerne alle Gefahren und Ungewissheiten ausschließen wollen.
Wegen der vielen Regeln leidet aber vor allem unsere Wirtschaft - es gibt mittlerweile viel so viele Vorschriften, das keiner mehr in der Lage ist alle zu überblicken. Die vielen Vorschriften schränken sich gegenseitig ein.
Und das Hauptproblem bei so vielen Vorschriften ist: Die Dinge werden irgendwann einfach sehr teuer.
Es ist allerings sehr bequem, wenn man für alles eine Vorschrift hat, muss man nicht denken -  oft aber gibt es Fälle, wo es noch keine Vorschrift gibt - dann fällt der gewöhnliche Deutsche in eine art Hilflosigkeitsstarre - er weis dann nicht, wie er handeln soll. 
Und ja, unser Humor lässt ein wenig zu wünschen übrig. Das Konzept ist uns noch ein wenig fremd. 
Eine kleine Bitte: lass doch bitte Neuschwanenstein weg. Das ist wirklich den Besuch nicht wert.

replies.author: @AlF-AB
replies.text:
I am German, and I have known the country since the 1970s. I can assure you that nothing is "declining." Of course, problems exist—as they always do. In my humble opinion, a country without problems has a much bigger problem.

The problems we are facing today are, among other things, the constant doomsayers (as always) and a growing number of people who simply cannot behave. These two groups seem to have found each other and now reinforce one another.

What you are telling us actually says more about your own host country than about Germany. Psychologically, it seems to be a case of projecting one country's reality onto another. Germany has improved tremendously over the past decades. Compared with the 1970s, 1980s, and even the 1990s, it is more tolerant, wealthier, and, in many respects, a better place to live.

replies.author: @duke6321
replies.text: @hfs9502 It almost hurts to have to read such nonsense. It is still excellent to live in Germany, not least because of the fact that Germans are also interested in the well-being of their fellow citizens and do not want to emulate the stilted need for recognition of many Americans. Employee rights are not an empty phrase here, but lived practice. Germans live in the community and not as lone wolves next to each other without being interested in their neighbours. In the USA, only the thickness of the wallet counts. People constantly believe that they have to impress their neighbors, so many U.S. citizens buy things on credit that they don't need in order to impress people they don't know. Most Germans don't like ostentation and bragging. And that's right.

comments.author: @AlF-AB
comments.text:
For your consideration - even if it is repeatedly claimed:

In Germany, it is not automatically an offense to cross a street outside of a marked pedestrian crossing (Zebrastreifen).

In Germany there is no specific rule requiring you to use the crosswalk, crossing elsewhere is usually not punishable.

Near a zebra crossing, pedestrians are generally expected to use it rather than crossing a few meters away, but crossing elsewhere is not, by itself, always an offense.

In the same way, a car driver is not obligated to halt if a pedestrian wants to cross at a zebra crossing. Hence, in case you observed that a lot of drivers - almost every driver! - stopped and let pedestrians cross, it might have the same reason why pedestrians opt to cross at zebra crossings: consideration.

In general, Germans tend to consider it very rude when someone behaves without consideration for others—for example, by speaking loudly on the phone in public (e.g. on buses, trains, or in waiting rooms), acting self-centered, constantly seeking attention, showing off, or dressing in a way that is overly flashy or inappropriate for the situation, especially when trying to display wealth they obviously do not have.

In line with this, even if you are verbally very polite and always smile warmly during interactions, many Germans will not perceive you as genuinely friendly if they feel that you lack consideration for those around you. Instead, they may see you as superficial or inauthentic, and over time they may begin to avoid interacting with you.

A German

replies.author: @AlF-AB
replies.text:
The people of Germany (similar to other nations in Europe) have their own national, regional, and even local cultures, depending on the region, city, town, village, and social milieu. They have been shaped by more than a thousand years of historical learning experiences: wars with virtually all of our neighbouring countries, civil wars—including the Thirty Years' War, which marked the beginning of the decline of the Holy Roman Empire of the German Nation and devastated large parts of the country, with some regions losing 30–50% of their population—and countless other events that shaped our society.

It is also a country with a relatively high population density, where unwritten social rules as well as laws and regulations are essential for millions of people to live together peacefully. What do you think is one of the reasons why people in countries such as Japan or China are often perceived as so respectful in public? Their neighbours are very often just an arm's length away.

Mutual respect and consideration are not only virtues—they are practical necessities, just like laws and regulations, and accepting the outcome of elections and political decisions taken by the elected representatives of the people.

Warm regards. I hope this was helpful.

replies.author: @AlF-AB
replies.text:
@reallifeincountries:
If the person standing next to you happens to be a traffic police officer, or someone who is particularly sensitive to the issue—for example because they have young children or work at an elementary school or kindergarten—they may decide to educate you about jaywalking. In general, however, most people simply do not care, especially in large, anonymous cities.

Note that we are talking about pedestrians, not cars. For drivers, the situation is completely different. Do not drive through a red light, even at night. While not every intersection is monitored by red-light cameras, some are. If you are caught, you can lose your driver's licence for a while and face a substantial fine.

What I do if I don't want to wait for the pedestrian light is simply continue walking in the direction I'm already heading. If I reach the road some distance away from the traffic lights, and the pedestrian light has meanwhile turned green and the cars have stopped, I cross there instead.

Even this can occasionally attract disapproving looks. People are different, situations are different, and places are different. Just be a little sensitive to the situation. For example, jaywalking right next to a police officer has a completely different meaning. It can easily be perceived as a personal insult or a deliberate provocation. Apart from that, police officers—like every civil servant—represent their host state, its authority, and its population. If you have lost respect for that, then leave. No problem. Not every country is for everybody. At the same time, German police are generally very restrained. Nobody rides you down with a horse, and nobody immediately reaches for a baton or a firearm. The same applies to drinking alcohol in public: it is usually tolerated as long as your behaviour is appropriate. So, in which countries are these supposedly law-obsessed and freedom-restricting people actually living instead?

Also note that in German we do not even have a commonly used word for jaywalking. That alone tells you that it is simply not regarded as a major issue in everyday life, despite what many foreigners believe. To me, it seems to be a much bigger issue in some English-speaking countries.

You can see this in other ways as well. In some countries, traffic wardens are needed outside schools every day. I have lived in places where they even have to stop traffic in both directions when school finishes. That is generally not necessary in Germany. Another example: how many speed humps, designed to slow cars down (and often damage them), have you seen in Germany? It simply is, or at least traditionally was, not considered necessary. This is also why I fear these Autobahn no-speed-limit enthusiasts. Too many people in this world are simply too poorly educated to handle the freedom they are given responsibly.

replies.author: @reallifeincountries
replies.text: Appreciate the sharp legal clarification! 🇩🇪 🚶‍♂️ Myths about German rules spread so fast. Is jaywalking actually treated pretty relaxed in daily life there, or do locals still give you 'the look' if you don't wait for the green man?

replies.author: @duke6321
replies.text: ​@reallifeincountriesIf you want to drive fast, you have to be considerate and highly concentrated at the same time so that you don't endanger yourself or others. The fact that there is no general speed limit here is not a myth, but a consequence of WW2. During the Nazi government, it was not allowed to exceed certain speed limits. Even on the motorway. In order to distance itself from the rules of the "Third Reich" in the liberal Federal Republic of Germany, the speed limit was also abolished - and has remained a constantly discussed topic of debate to this day. Today for ecological reasons and because of environmental protection.

replies.author: @reallifeincountries
replies.text: You hit the nail on the head! 🏎️ 🇩🇪 The 'no speed limit' myth often overshadows the incredibly strict lane discipline and laws that actually make the Autobahn safe. Why do you think outsiders struggle to understand that true freedom on the road requires even stricter rules?"
